learning_outcomes.description: Master a range of attributes from both the research and presentation aspects of this course, as well as the written assessment, including information management, communication, critical thinking, cultural and historical appreciation, ethics, and an interdisciplinary perspective.
Work in an independent, adaptive and self-motivated fashion, as well as interact effectively with others for a common outcome.
Develop a sound understanding of the major issues relating to comparative genocide research and the important factors which shape this field of research.
Have an appreciation of the ethical and philosophical contexts related to genocides and acts of persecution.
Understand the historical contexts in which genocide has taken place.

assessments.due_date:
5/08/2026 - 14/10/2026

The tutorial presentations for the semester will be scheduled in Week 2's tutorial.
assessments.task_description:
Tutorial Participation consists of:


(i) Weekly attendance and participation in the ten tutorials scheduled for the semester (10%)

Students are required to attend tutorials, having read the assigned tutorial readings, and actively participate in the discussion of issues raised in the lectures and tutorial readings.

Note: Tutorials start in Week 2.


(ii) Tutorial Presentation (10%)

Each student is required to give an oral presentation of no more than 6 minutes on a theme covered in the tutorial program. Students may choose to answer one or more of the tutorial questions, or focus on another issue that is directly related to the tutorial topic. The emphasis in the tutorial presentation is on not only understanding a particular topic but also on having the ability to communicate that understanding to an audience in an interesting and persuasive manner.


1. Presentation should be no longer than 6 minutes.

2. Presentation must address the topic for that week. 

3. Presentations should NOT BE READ. Speak to the class from your notes, rather than read from them.

4. Presentations should be done with enthusiasm! Be upbeat, but not casual.

5. Presentations should be intellectually stimulating and entertaining.

6. Focus on the main points. Don't go into too much detail. A presentation is not a research essay.

7. Presentations should be based on the assigned readings as well as other scholarly sources related to the topic.

8. Presentations should be well-structured: a clear introduction; two or three key points; and a clear conclusion.

9. A brief introduction and conclusion to your talk are important. Give them some thought.

10. Nervousness is normal! Use it to enhance your presentation.

11. Anyone can be a good public speaker. Like everything, all it needs is practice.


Note: Tutorials start in Week 2. The presentation schedule will be organized in Week 2's tutorial. Presentations will start in Week 3
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Your task for this assignment is to write an essay of 1000 words on the following topic:

What are the strengths and weaknesses of the 1948 UN Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide. To what extent is the Convention limited by the political and historical circumstances when it was produced? Can the definition of genocide change over time?


Make sure that:

1. The essay answers the question;

2. The essay is sufficiently researched, based primarily on the historical scholarly literature;

3. The essay is written in clear, concise, persuasive and grammatically correct prose;

4. The essay has a clear structure: a clear introduction in which the problem is introduced; a main body with a logical organization of ideas arranged into discreet paragraphs; and a conclusion in which you sum up your response to the essay question;

5. The essay has sufficient referencing and uses proper referencing standards. Footnotes must include the page number.

6. Word length: 1000 words +/- 10%. Footnotes and bibliography are not included in word limit.


Referencing guide: Use Chicago 18A style: https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/referencing/chicago-18th-edition-notes-and-bibliography

NOTE: This assessment must be submitted in order to pass the course.


Use of AI: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Academic Misconduct - Referencing: Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

NOTE: This assessment must be submitted in order to pass the course.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The major assignment in this course is to address one of the questions below in ONE of the following three forms: (i) Essay; (ii) Podcast; (iii) Documentary-style movie (using Apple's iMovie or Windows' Movie Maker, etc.).


The length requirement of each format is as follows:

1. Essay: 2500 words, +/- 10% (for referencing use Chicago style; footnotes and bibliography are not included in word limit).

2. Podcast: 12-15 minutes

3. Documentary-style movie: 12-15 minutes


QUESTIONS

1. Discuss the origins and development of genocide studies as a field of study. In what ways has the field changed over time, and why?

2. Did the mass killings of ethnic Armenians in 1915-16 by the Ottoman Turks constitute a genocide? Why is this history so controversial?

3 . What are the legal and philosophical differences between Raphael Lemkin's concept of 'genocide' and Hersch Lauterpacht's concept of 'crimes against humanity'.

4. Discuss the differences between the legal definition of 'genocide' in the 1948 Genocide Convention and the various scholarly definitions of genocide.

5. Why is the debate in the scholarly literature between the "intentionalist" and the "functionalist" explanations of the Holocaust so controversial?

6. How distinctive is the Holocaust in the history of genocide? Argue with reference both to the Holocaust and other cases of genocide in the twentieth century.

7. Do incidents in the historical treatment of Australia's Indigenous peoples by settlers constitute 'genocide'? Discuss the historiographical debate on this question.

8. Evaluate the achievements and shortcomings of the International Tribunals in the former Yugoslavia, Rwanda, or Cambodia.

9. Were the war crimes trials in Nuremberg or Tokyo after the Second World War anything more than 'victors’ justice'?

10. Explain the phenomenon of genocide denial in relation to the Armenian genocide, the Holocaust, or another incident of your choice. 

11. Evaluate the role of the United Nations in preventing genocide.

12. Can the great famines in Ukraine under the USSR in 1932-33, or in the PRC during the 'Great Leap Forward' from 1958-62, be regarded as genocide? Choose either USSR or the PRC.

13. To what extent was decolonization a crucial factor in genocide and crimes against humanity between the 1950s and 1970s?. Answer with reference to one case study.

14. What factors were responsible for the mass killings in Cambodia under the Khmer Rouge regime (1975-78)? Discuss the role of Cold War politics in influencing the history of this period. Did these mass killings constitute 'genocide'?

15. Why has the history of the 1965-66 mass killings in Indonesia been largely suppressed? What are the reasons for the recently renewed scholarly interest in this historical incident?

16. Why was the United States government unwilling to intervene in the genocide in East Pakistan / Bangladesh in 1971?

17. Explain the process whereby modern states and ordinary people can willingly take part in genocide or crimes against humanity (you may focus on one or more countries).

18. Discuss the role of gender and sexualised violence in one case of genocide or a crime against humanity.

19. Choose one artist, writer, book or film and discuss his/her/its representation of an incident of genocide (examples include East-West Street, The Diary of Anne Frank, Schindler's List, Sophie's Choice, Maus, The Killing Fields, Hotel Rwanda, The Act of Killing).

20. Choose one or more forms of transitional justice such as a Truth Commission, an International Tribunal, or indigenous jurisprudence. Discuss the extent to which the process provides justice for the victims of mass atrocities. You may focus on one or more countries.

21. Why does it appear so difficult for the international community to intervene to prevent a genocide from taking place? Answer with reference to one case of genocide.

22. How did the doctrine of the 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P) develop, and how successful has it been?

23. Why have Japanese governments found it so difficult to acknowledge responsibility for war crimes committed in the Pacific War between 1937 and 1945?

24. Account for the rise of 'genocide recognition politics' since the 1990s.


Referencing guide: https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/referencing/chicago-18th-edition-notes-and-bibliography

NOTE: This assessment must be submitted in order to pass the course.


Use of AI: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Academic Misconduct - Referencing: Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

assessments.task_description:
A 120-minute examination will be held during the examinations period. Students will be required to answer multiple choice and short-answer questions and write an extended essay chosen from a list of questions. Examination questions will be taken from material covered in lectures, tutorial discussions, and the assigned readings from the course.

NOTE: Students must take the examination in order to pass the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: The Origins of Genocide Studies

This lecture presents an overview of the development of the field of Genocide Studies in the second half of the twentieth century. The lecture will also go over the course organization, administration, the lecture and tutorial topics, readings, and assessment.


There's no tutorial this week, but try to do this reading:

Ervin Staub, "The Origins of Genocide and Mass Killing: Core Concepts", in Samuel Totten and Paul Re. Bartrop, eds., The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London: Routledge, 2009), pp. 97-107.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

The lecture will also go over the course organization, administration, the lecture and tutorial topics, readings, and assessment. There's no tutorial this week , but try to do this reading: Ervin Staub, "The Origins of Genocide and Mass Killing: Core Concepts", in Samuel Totten and Paul Re. Bartrop, eds., The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London: Routledge, 2009), pp. 97-107.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Nazi Germany and the Holocaust: The 'Model' Genocide

Lecture 2: Nazi Germany and the Holocaust: The ‘Model’ Genocide

This week's lecture discusses the Holocaust: the genocide of the Jewish population in Europe during the Second World War. It looks at debates over the various causes of the Holocaust. It explains why the Holocaust is the best-known genocide of the twentieth century, to the extent that it has been posited as the 'model genocide', that is, the genocide by which other genocides are compared.


Week 2 Tutorial Questions: 

Introduction and organization of tutorial presentations for the semester.

In what sense is the genocide of the Jews by Nazi Germany the 'model genocide'? How have different historians attempted to explain the reasons for the Holocaust? Is it proper to commemorate the extermination of Gypsies, Romani, homosexuals and the mentally disabled as part of the Holocaust? Why is the Holocaust the best-known genocide of the twentieth century? Is the Holocaust distinct from other genocides? In what way?


Readings

"Reich Citizens Law of September 15, 1935", and "Nuremberg Law for the Protection of the German Blood and of the German Honour of 15 September 1935"; Office of the Historian, Foreign Relations of the United States, Diplomatic Papers, 1935, Volume II, The British Commonwealth; Europe, Document 305 <https://history.state.gov/historicaldocuments/frus1935v02/d305 >

Franklin Bialystock, “The Holocaust”, in William L. Hewitt., ed., Defining the Horrific: Readings on Genocide and Holocaust in the Twentieth Century (Upper Saddle River NJ: Pearson-Prentice Hall, 2004), pp. 142-159.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Defining the Crime of Genocide: Lemkin, the UN, the Superpowers, and Lemkin's Critics

Lecture 3: Defining the Crime of Genocide: Lemkin, the UN, the Superpowers, and Lemkin's Critics

This week's lecture examines the origins of the legal concept of 'genocide' coined by the Polish-Jewish lawyer, Raphael Lemkin, in 1944. The lecture places Lemkin's thinking within a longer tradition of international law that sought to regulate war and the treatment of civilians. The lecture examines how Lemkin's concept of 'genocide' eventually led to the 1948 UN Convention of the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide. The lecture also considers the development of another legal concept of 'crimes against humanity', and criticisms of Lemkin. The lecture also discusses the tension between the principle of national sovereignty and the emerging doctrine of universal human rights.


Week 3 Tutorial Questions: How did great power politics after the end of the Second World War shape the definition of the crime of 'genocide'? What problems are there with the definition of genocide in the 1948 UN Convention of the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide? Why has the UN Genocide Convention not been more effective? What are the legal and philosophical difference between the concepts of 'genocide' and 'crimes against humanity'? Should the legal definition of genocide take precedence over other definitions? Does it matter whether mass killings are referred to as 'genocide' rather than by another term? Discuss the tension between the principle of national sovereignty and 'humanitarian intervention' by the 'international community'.


Readings

Office of the High Commissioner, United Nations Human Rights, “UN Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide” 9 December 1948 <https://www.ohchr.org/en/instruments-mechanisms/instruments/convention-prevention-and-punishment-crime-genocide>

“The Issues of Sovereignty and Political Will”, in Samuel Totten and Paul R. Bartrop, eds., The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London: Routledge, 2009), pp. 287-297.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 3: Defining the Crime of Genocide: Lemkin, the UN, the Superpowers, and Lemkin's Critics

Bartrop, eds., The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London: Routledge, 2009), pp. 287-297.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Victor's Justice? War Crimes Trials in Europe and Asia after World War II

Lecture 4: Victor's Justice? War Crimes Trials in Europe and Asia 

This week's lecture looks at two of the most famous criminal tribunals of the twentieth century: the Nuremberg and Tokyo War Crimes Trials. The lecture discusses what kind of justice the legal system can provide for the victims of genocide and crimes against humanity. It will evaluate the quality of the legal processes that were used during the trials. It will discuss the question as to whether these trials were purely 'victor's justice'. The lecture will also discuss the vexed question of individual responsibility for crimes committed in the context of war.


Week 4 Tutorial Questions: “The post-war War Crimes trials in Nuremberg and Tokyo were nothing more than ‘victors’ justice’”. Argue for or against. Discuss the similarities and differences between the war crimes trials in Nuremberg and Tokyo. What problems were there with the legality of the trials? How successful were these trials in setting a precedent for the prosecution of later perpetrators of genocide and war crimes?


Readings

“Statement by Justice Jackson on War Trials Agreement: August 12, 1945” <https://avalon.law.yale.edu/imt/imt_jack02.asp> and "“Nuremberg Trial Proceedings Vol. 1: Charter of the International Military Tribunal” <https://avalon.law.yale.edu/imt/imtconst.asp>, cited in The Avalon Project: Documents in Law, History and Diplomacy, Yale Law School (2008)

Charter, International Military Tribunal for the Far East, US Naval War College Digital Commons, 19 January 1946 <https://digital-commons.usnwc.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2129&context=ils>

“The International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg: 1945-1946”, in Howard Ball, Prosecuting War Crimes and Genocide: The Twentieth Century Experience (Kansas City: University of Kansas Press, 1999), pp. 53 – 61; pp. 73 – 79.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 4: Victor's Justice? War Crimes Trials in Europe and Asia after World War II

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: The 'Banality of Evil': How Ordinary People Become Complicit in Mass Killings

Lecture 5: The 'Banality of Evil': How Ordinary People Become Complicit in Mass Killings

In 1963 the philosopher Hannah Arendt published a book about the trial of Nazi war criminal, Adolf Eichmann, titled Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil . In the book Arendt controversially described Eichmann's actions as "the banality of evil". In doing so Arendt opened up a debate about how ordinary people could be motivated to take part in genocide and mass killings. This lecture examines Arendt's argument and subsequent attempts by social psychologists to understand how ordinary people can become perpetrators of violence and killing.


Week 5 Tutorial Questions: What did the political philosopher Hannah Arendt mean by the phrase, the 'banality of evil'? Why was Arendt's observation controversial? Why was Browning's argument in his 1992 book, Ordinary Men: Reserve Police Battalion 101 and the Final Solution in Poland, so provocative? What do the Milgram experiments suggest about the potential of ordinary people to inflict violence on people under their authority?


Readings

Hannah Arendt, Eichmann in Jerusalem: a report on the Banality of Evil (New York and London: Penguin: 1994), pp. 287-9.

"Biography of Adolf Eichmann" from ‘Adolf Eichmann: The Functionary and the Symbol’, in Israel W. Charny, (ed.), Encyclopaedia of Genocide Volume I, A-H, Santa Barbara, Cal.; Denver Col., Oxford, England: ABC-CLIO, 1999, pp. 208–13.

“The Psychology of Perpetrators”, in Adam Jones, Genocide: a Comprehensive Introduction (London and New York: Routledge, 2006), pp. 270-275.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 5: The 'Banality of Evil': How Ordinary People Become Complicit in Mass Killings

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Genocide Denial: The Armenians, the Holocaust, and Beyond

Lecture 6: Genocide Denial: The Armenians, the Holocaust, and Beyond

The killing of up to 1.5 million Armenians by the government of the Ottoman Empire between 1915 and 1917 was arguably the greatest genocide of the early twentieth century. Later Turkish governments have gone to great lengths to deny that a genocide ever took place. This lecture examines the phenomenon of genocide denial, taking the case of the Armenians and the Holocaust as case studies. The lecture examines how genocide denial takes place, and how it can be understood as a part of the process of genocide itself. The lecture also examines the question to what extent the principle of free speech should tolerate genocide denial.


Week 6 Tutorial Questions: Explain the phenomenon of genocide denial, identifying cases from around the world. Discuss the different forms of genocide denial. Choose an instance of genocide denial and identify the motivation and processes of the deniers. Should genocide denial be made illegal? Why/why not? How have victims of genocide or crimes against humanity themselves attempted to preserve the history of such events? Discuss the recent trend of 'genocide recognition politics' - the process by which governments recognise mass killing events in the past as 'genocide'.


Readings

"Biden becomes first US president to recognise Armenian genocide” The Guardian, 25 April 2021 <https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2021/apr/24/joe-biden-armenian-genocide-recognition>

Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Türkiye, “The Events of 1915 and the Turkish-Armenian Controversy over History: An Overview” (2022) https://www.mfa.gov.tr/the-events-of-1915-and-the-turkish-armenian-controversy-over-history_-an-overview.en.mfa

Richard G. Hovannisian, “The Armenian Genocide and Patterns of Denial”, in Richard G. Hovannisian, ed., Armenian Genocide in Perspective (Routledge, 1986), pp. 111-33.

“Denial of Genocide”, in The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London: Routledge, 2009), pp. 517-531.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 6: Genocide Denial: The Armenians, the Holocaust, and Beyond

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Genocide, Colonialism, and Indigenous Peoples: The Case of the Australian Aborigines

Lecture 7: Genocide, Colonialism, and Indigenous Peoples: The Case of the Australian Aborigines  

This week's lecture examines the controversial debate as to whether settler violence against and dispossession of Australia's indigenous peoples can be classified as genocide. The lecture looks at how Raphael Lemkin viewed the question of genocide in colonial Australia in his writings. It also looks at how activists, historians, and lawyers in Australia have dealt with this question, including the controversial 1997 Bringing Them Home report into the separation of indigenous children from their parents. The lecture also discusses the growing historical attention to the connection between colonization and genocide of indigenous peoples in other parts of the world.


Week 7 Tutorial Questions: How did Raphael Lemkin view the question of genocide and indigenous peoples? How have indigenous activist groups and historians viewed the issue of genocide in the history of indigenous peoples in Australia since the 1960s? What was the view of the Australian historian of frontier violence, Henry Reynolds, on the question of genocide? Discuss the issue of 'intent' in debates about genocide and indigenous peoples.


Readings

“Genocides of Indigenous Peoples”, in Adam Jones, Genocide: a Comprehensive Introduction (London and New York: Routledge, 2006), pp. 67-86.

Ward Churchill, A Little Matter of Genocide; Holocaust and Denial in the Americas, 1492 to the Present (San Francisco: City Lights Books, 1997), pp. 1-12.

A. Dirk Moses, 'Genocide and Holocaust Consciousness in Australia', History Compass 1.1 (2003): 1-13.

Tony Barta, 'After the Holocaust: Consciousness of Genocide in Australia', The Australian Journal of Politics and History, vol. 31, no. 1 (1985): 154-61


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 7: Genocide, Colonialism, and Indigenous Peoples: The Case of the Australian Aborigines
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Genocide, Colonialism, and Indigenous Peoples: The Case of the Australian Aborigines Lecture 7: Genocide, Colonialism, and Indigenous Peoples: The Case of the Australian Aborigines This week's lecture examines the controversial debate as to whether settler violence against and dispossession of Australia's indigenous peoples can be classified as genocide. The lecture looks at how Raphael Lemkin viewed the question of genocide in colonial Australia in his writings. It also looks at how activists, historians, and lawyers in Australia have dealt with this question, including the controversial 1997 Bringing Them Home report into the separation of indigenous children from their parents. The lecture also discusses the growing historical attention to the connection between colonization and genocide of indigenous peoples in other parts of the world. Week 7 Tutorial Questions : How did Raphael Lemkin view the question of genocide and indigenous peoples? How have indigenous activist groups and historians viewed the issue of genocide in the history of indigenous peoples in Australia since the 1960s? What was the view of the Australian historian of frontier violence, Henry Reynolds, on the question of genocide? Discuss the issue of 'intent' in debates about genocide and indigenous peoples. Readings “Genocides of Indigenous Peoples”, in Adam Jones, Genocide: a Comprehensive Introduction (London and New York: Routledge, 2006), pp. 67-86. Ward Churchill, A Little Matter of Genocide; Holocaust and Denial in the Americas, 1492 to the Present (San Francisco: City Lights Books, 1997), pp. 1-12. A. Dirk Moses, 'Genocide and Holocaust Consciousness in Australia', History Compass 1.1 (2003): 1-13. Tony Barta, 'After the Holocaust: Consciousness of Genocide in Australia', The Australian Journal of Politics and History , vol. 31, no. 1 (1985): 154-61

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Genocide in the Cold War: The Indonesian Killings of 1965-66 and the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia

Lecture 8: Genocide in the Cold War: The Indonesian Killings of 1965-66 and the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia

The Cold War in the period between 1947 and 1991 contributed to the political polarization of many countries around the world. Such polarization resulted in enormous violence and numerous cases of genocide or mass killing perpetrated by both the political right and the left. This lecture looks at two of the worse cases: the mass killing of 500,000 - 1,000 000 members or suspected members of the Indonesian Communist Party (PKI) in 1965-66, and the deaths through execution, famine, and disease of perhaps a quarter of Cambodia's population under the radical Khmer Rouge government between 1975 and December 1978. The lecture also gives attention to the subject of sexual violence in genocide, and how men and women are targeted differently.


Week 8 Tutorial Questions: How did communist revolutions create conditions in which genocide or crimes against humanity could take place? Choose an example of mass killing perpetrated by a communist regime and consider whether ideology was the prime motivator in the violence. What other factors were involved besides ideology? Discuss the process by which the great famine in Ukraine in 1932-33 during the Soviet period was classified as 'genocide'. The UN Genocide Convention's definition of genocide omitted 'political groups' from its list of targetted groups. How significant was this omission in the context of the Cold War? How useful is the term “politicide” for labelling such mass killings? 


Readings

Robert Cribb, "Genocide in Indonesia 1965-1966", Journal of Genocide Research, 3:2 (2001): 219-239.

Annie Pohlman, “The Spectre of Communist Women, Sexual Violence and Citizenship in Indonesia” Sexualities, Vol. 20, 1-2 (2017), pp. 196-211.

"Gendering Genocide”, in Adam Jones, Genocide: a Comprehensive Introduction (London and New York: Routledge, 2006), pp. 325-336.

Ben Kiernan, “The Cambodian Genocide 1975-1979”, in Samuel Totten and William S. Parsons, eds., Century of Genocide: Critical Essays and Eyewitness Accounts (New York and London: Routledge, 2013), pp. 317-350.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: The 'Blood Telegram' and the Forgotten Genocide in Bangladesh

Lecture 9: The 'Blood Telegram' and the Forgotten Genocide in Bangladesh

In the 1947 'Partition' of India a Muslim-majority state of Pakistan was created amidst massive communal violence. However relations between 'West Pakistan' and the predominantly Bengali-speaking 'East Pakistan' quickly broke down. The resulting crisis led to what is understood today as a 'forgotten genocide' in 1971, when West Pakistan armed forces invaded East Pakistan and killed up to three million Bengalis and raped hundreds of thousands of Bengali women and girls. The crisis led to an independence movement that created the new state of Bangladesh. This lecture examines one of the worst, but least acknowledged genocides of the twentieth century. It also evaluates the claim that the United States administration was complicit in the genocide after it was informed by its Consulate in Dhaka of what was happening but failed to take any action to prevent it.


Week 8 Tutorial Questions: Why did relations between West and East Pakistan breakdown so quickly after Partition in 1947? What role did India play in the crisis? Why did the US government support the Pakistan government in its actions in East Pakistan?


Readings

'Telegram from the Consulate General in Dacca to the Department of State', Foreign Relations of the United States, 1969-76, Volume XI, South Asia Crisis, Office of the Historian, Dacca April 6, 1971: https://history.state.gov/historicaldocuments/frus1969-76v11/d19

Gary Bass, 'Preface', The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide (New York: Alfred A Knopf, 2013), pp. xi-xxv.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 9: The 'Blood Telegram' and the Forgotten Genocide in Bangladesh

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: The Path to Reconciliation: The Origins of 'Transitional Justice'

Lecture 10: The Path to Reconciliation: The Origins of 'Transitional Justice'

Beginning in the 1970s - 1980s scholars, human rights activists, and international organizations like the UN began to develop the concept of 'transitional justice' for societies that had experienced large-scale human rights abuses over long periods of time. The concept refers to a range of processes and mechanisms including, but not limited to, legal proceedings, 'to ensure accountability, serve justice, and achieve reconciliation' (United Nations). This lecture explains and evaluates some of these mechanisms, including Truth and Reconciliation Commissions, indigenous forms of justice, reparations and compensation, and commemoration and memorialization.


Week 10 Tutorial Questions: How 'just' and 'useful' are Truth and Justice Commissions? Answer with reference to at least one historical example of such a commission. Discuss the tension between the search for justice for victims of genocide or mass atrocities, and the desire for 'national reconciliation'. Explain the concept of 'transitional justice'. Evaluate the arguments for and against 'transitional justice'. To what extent is the issue of reconciliation a political, rather than a moral, problem? How important to the process of reconciliation is preserving the history and memory of mass killings? How should genocide or crimes against humanity be remembered? 


Readings

"What is Transitional Justice?" International Center for Transitional Justice (2008) <chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://legal.un.org/avl/pdf/ls/Van-Zyl_RecReading1_.pdf>

Ernesto Verdeja, “Transitional Justice and Genocide”, in Joyce Apsel and Ernesto Verdeja.. eds., Genocide Matters: Ongoing Issues and Emerging Perspectives (London and New York: Routledge, 2013), pp. 172-94.

Kenneth Christie, The South African Truth Commission (Basingstoke, UK: Macmillan; New York: St Martin’s Press, 2000), pp. 54-64; 120-4.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: The Return of Genocide: Rwanda and the former Yugoslavia in the 1990s

Lecture 11: The Return of Genocide: Rwanda and the former Yugoslavia in the 1990s

Following the end of the Cold War in 1991 there was great optimism about a coming more peaceful 'New World Order'. Such optimism was shattered by two incidents in the early 1990s: a genocide in the small central African nation of Rwanda, a former Belgian colony, and the civil war in the former Yugoslavia, and a genocide carried out against Bosnian Muslims. The reoccurrence of genocide, and the seeming impotence of the international community to prevent it, forced scholars to revised their understanding of genocide, and to try to develop political mechanisms that would prevent genocides from taking place.


Week 11 Tutorial Questions: Discuss the origin of the concept of 'ethnic cleansing'. Why has ethnicity been so politicized in the twentieth century? Choose an example of genocide in either the former Yugoslavia or Rwanda and discuss the reasons why ethnicity played such an important role in the violence. How was ethnic identity defined in the cases of the former Yugoslavia and Rwanda? What other factors contributed to the genocide in these two cases? Discuss the historical roots of the enmity between different ethnic groups in these two countries. How important is this history in explaining the violence? How can this history be overcome?


Readings

Robert Melson, “Modern Genocide in Rwanda: Revolution, War, and Mass Murder in an African State”, in Robert Gellately and Ben Kiernan, eds., The Spectre of Genocide: Mass Murder in Historical Perspective (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2003), pp. 325-338.

“Genocidal Violence in the Former Yugoslavia: Bosnia Herzegovina” in Samuel Totten and William S. Parsons, eds., Centuries of Genocide: Essays and Eyewitness Accounts, 4th ed. (New York and London, Routledge: 2013), pp. 476-89.

Anthony Marino, “Bosnia v. Serbia and the Status of Rape as Genocide under International Law”, Boston University International Law Journal, Vol. 27 Issue 1, 2009, pp. 205-229.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Genocide Prevention and the Rise and Fall of 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P)

Lecture 12: Genocide Prevention and the Rise and Fall of 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P)

This week's lecture traces the development of thinking about how to anticipate and prevent genocide from taking place. The story begins with the work of scholars and scholar-activists in the 1960s - 1990s and culminates with the United Nations' Member States' adoption of the doctrine of 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P) in 2005, which created a mechanism for the 'international community' to intervene in situations where states were unable or unwilling to protect their own populations. The lecture evaluates the doctrine of R2P in the wake of conflicts in Libya in 2011 and most recently, Gaza. The lecture returns to consider the long-standing tension between the principal of state sovereignty and humanitarian intervention.


No Tutorial


Recommended Readings

Israel W. Charney, ‘Israel Charney’s Genocide Early Warning Processes', in Israel W. Charney (ed.), Encyclopaedia of Genocide, V. 1 (Santa Barbara, Cal.: ABC-CLIO, 1999), pp. 257-260.

Gregory H. Stanton, 'The Eight Stages of Genocide”, in Genocide Watch: the International Alliance to End Genocide <http://www.genocidewatch.org/genocide/8stagesofgenocide.html>

'About the Responsibility to Protect', Office on Genocide Prevention and the Responsibility to Protect, United Nations (2025) <https://www.un.org/en/genocide-prevention/responsibility-protect/about>

'The Rise and Fall of the Responsibility to Protect', Council on Foreign Relations, April 20, 2023 <https://education.cfr.org/learn/timeline/rise-and-fall-responsibility-protect>

Barbara Harff, 'The Etiology of Genocides', in Samuel Totten and Paul R. Bartrop, eds., The Genocide Studies Reader (New York and London, Routledge: 2009), pp. 108-120.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: 'Never Again'? Palestine, Israel, and Genocide in Gaza

Lecture 13: 'Never Again'? Palestine, Israel, and Genocide in Gaza

The study of genocide began with research on the Holocaust following the Second World War. By the 1990s it had become a fully-fledged academic field, dedicated not only to studying genocide but in doing so to prevent it from taking place again. As of 2026 a consensus has developed amongst journalists, genocide scholars, and United Nations officials, that Israel's actions in Gaza since the Hamas attack on Israelis on October 7 2023 constitutes genocide. The genocide in Gaza has thus profoundly affected the field of genocide studies, leading some scholars to question the foundations of the field itself.


No Tutorial


Recommended Reading

International Association of Genocide Scholars, “Resolution on the Situation in Gaza”, 31 August 2025 <https://genocidescholars.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/IAGS-Resolution-on-Gaza-FINAL.pdf>

Refaat Alareer, “If I Must Die” (2023) <https://ifimustdie.net/>

Israel Charney, "Gaza Fight is Humanly and Legally Just but Deserves Ceasefire if Hostages Released", The Israel Times, February 2 2024: https://blogs.timesofisrael.com/gaza-fight-is-humanly-and-legally-just-but-deserves-ceasefire-if-hostages-freed/

Imad K. Harb, Review, Susan Abulhawa, ed., Every Moment Is a Life: Gaza in the Time of Genocide (New York: Atria/One Signal Publishers 2026), Arab Center Washington DC, February 2 2026 <https://arabcenterdc.org/resource/every-moment-is-a-life-gaza-in-the-time-of-genocide/> 

Peter Beinart, Being Jewish after the Destruction of Gaza: A Reckoning (Atlantic Books, 2025)

Ilan Pappe, The Ethnic Cleansing of Palestine (Oneworld Publications, 2011)


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
